annotations.label: Eigenvector and eigenvalue (informal)

anchor.snippet: an eigenvector ( / ˈ aɪ ɡ ən -/ EYE -gən- ) or characteristic vector is a (nonzero) vector that has its direction unchanged (or reversed) by a given linear transformation

annotations.note: `HasEigenvector f μ x` requires `x ∈ eigenspace f μ ∧ x ≠ 0`, capturing the informal notion of a nonzero vector whose direction is preserved.

annotations.label: Eigenvalue (corresponding factor)

anchor.snippet: The corresponding eigenvalue , characteristic value , or characteristic root is the multiplying factor

annotations.note: `HasEigenvalue f μ` is the scaling factor of an eigenvector.

annotations.label: Eigenvector of a matrix

anchor.snippet: For an [MATH] matrix A and a nonzero

annotations.note: Matrix eigenvectors are handled by applying `HasEigenvector` to `Matrix.toLin'` or `toLin b b`, e.g. `hasEigenvector_toLin'_diagonal`.

annotations.label: Equivalence of matrix and linear-transformation definitions

anchor.snippet: it is equivalent to define eigenvalues and eigenvectors using either the language of linear transformations, or the language of matrices

annotations.note: `LinearMap.spectrum_toMatrix` and `Matrix.spectrum_toLin` show the spectra agree under `toMatrix`/`toLin`.

annotations.label: Eigenvalue equation (eigenequation)

anchor.snippet: referred to as the eigenvalue equation or eigenequation

annotations.note: `mem_eigenspace_iff` states `x ∈ eigenspace f μ ↔ f x = μ • x`, which is the eigenequation.

anchors.snippet: referred to as the eigenvalue equation or eigenequation

anchor.snippet: The example here, based on the Mona Lisa , provides a simple illustration

annotations.note: This is an illustrative pictorial example not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Eigenfunctions of a differential operator

anchor.snippet: the linear transformation could be a differential operator

annotations.note: Eigenfunctions are special cases of `HasEigenvector` for a linear differential operator, but no dedicated `eigenfunction` API exists.

anchors.snippet: the linear transformation could be a differential operator

annotations.label: Eigensystem, eigenspace, eigenbasis

anchor.snippet: The set of all eigenvectors of a linear transformation, each paired with its corresponding eigenvalue, is called the eigensystem of that transformation

annotations.note: `eigenspace` and `Eigenvalues` are defined; `Matrix.IsHermitian.eigenvectorBasis` gives an eigenbasis in the Hermitian case, but no general `eigenbasis`/`eigensystem` term.

annotations.label: Scalar multiples (parallel/collinear vectors)

anchor.snippet: These vectors are said to be scalar multiples of each other, or parallel or collinear

annotations.note: Scalar multiplication via `SMul`/`HSMul` is core Mathlib; `SameRay` formalizes parallel vectors.

anchors.snippet: These vectors are said to be scalar multiples of each other, or parallel or collinear

anchor.snippet: then v is an eigenvector of the linear transformation A and the scale factor λ is the eigenvalue corresponding to that eigenvector

annotations.note: Same as the general definition, applied via `Matrix.toLin'`; see `hasEigenvector_toLin'_diagonal`.

annotations.note: `eigenspace_def` shows `eigenspace f μ = LinearMap.ker (f - μ • 1)`, i.e. `(A - λI)v = 0`.

annotations.label: Characteristic equation via determinant

anchor.snippet: has a nonzero solution v if and only if the determinant of the matrix

annotations.note: Eigenvalues are exactly the roots of the characteristic polynomial; equivalently `det(λI − A) = 0` via `eval_charpoly`.

annotations.label: Characteristic polynomial

anchor.snippet: This polynomial is called the characteristic polynomial of A

annotations.note: Both `LinearMap.charpoly` and `Matrix.charpoly` are defined.

annotations.label: Factoring the characteristic polynomial

anchor.snippet: has at most n complex number roots, which can be found by factoring the characteristic polynomial

annotations.note: `charpoly_natDegree` gives degree `n`; `Polynomial.card_roots_le_degree` bounds the roots.

anchor.snippet: As a brief example, which is described in more detail in the examples section later

annotations.label: Algebraic numbers entries give algebraic eigenvalues

anchor.snippet: if the entries of A are all algebraic numbers , which include the rationals, the eigenvalues must also be algebraic numbers

annotations.note: Mathlib proves `f` is integral over `R` via `charpoly`; the precise corollary about algebraic eigenvalues is not stated directly.

annotations.label: Complex conjugate eigenvalue pairs

anchor.snippet: The non-real roots of a real polynomial with real coefficients can be grouped into pairs of complex conjugates

annotations.note: General fact about conjugate roots of real polynomials exists; not specialized to eigenvalues.

annotations.label: Spectrum of a matrix

anchor.snippet: The spectrum of a matrix is the list of eigenvalues, repeated according to multiplicity

annotations.note: `spectrum R a` is defined; specialized to matrices via `Matrix.mem_spectrum_iff_isRoot_charpoly`.

annotations.label: Spectral radius

anchor.snippet: This is known as the spectral radius of the matrix

annotations.note: `spectralRadius 𝕜 a` is the supremum of `‖k‖₊` over `k ∈ spectrum 𝕜 a`.

annotations.label: Algebraic multiplicity

anchor.snippet: The algebraic multiplicity

annotations.note: `rootMultiplicity` of `charpoly` gives algebraic multiplicity but no dedicated `algebraicMultiplicity` for eigenvalues.

annotations.label: Simple and semisimple eigenvalue

anchor.snippet: then λ i is said to be a simple eigenvalue

annotations.note: `IsSemisimple` is for endomorphisms (not single eigenvalues); no `simpleEigenvalue` predicate.

annotations.note: `eigenspace` applied to `Matrix.toLin'`; e.g. `iSup_eigenspace_toLin'_diagonal_eq_top`.

annotations.label: Eigenspace is closed under addition and scalar multiplication

anchor.section: Eigenspaces, geometric multiplicity, and the eigenbasis for matrices
anchor.snippet: Because the eigenspace E is a linear subspace, it is closed under addition

annotations.note: By construction `eigenspace f μ` has type `Submodule R M`, hence closed under addition and scalar multiplication.

anchor.section: Eigenspaces, geometric multiplicity, and the eigenbasis for matrices
anchor.snippet: is referred to as the eigenvalue's geometric multiplicity

annotations.note: Expressible as `finrank K (eigenspace f μ)`; no dedicated `geometricMultiplicity` definition.

anchors.snippet: is referred to as the eigenvalue's geometric multiplicity

annotations.label: Eigenbasis from total geometric multiplicity

anchor.section: Eigenspaces, geometric multiplicity, and the eigenbasis for matrices
anchor.snippet: The direct sum of the eigenspaces of all of A 's eigenvalues is the entire vector space

annotations.note: For semisimple endomorphisms over an algebraically closed field the eigenspaces span; combined with `eigenspaces_iSupIndep` they form a direct sum decomposition.

annotations.label: Trace equals sum of eigenvalues

anchor.snippet: The trace of A , defined as the sum of its diagonal elements, is also the sum of all eigenvalues

annotations.note: Stated in terms of `charpoly.roots`; `Matrix.IsHermitian.trace_eq_sum_eigenvalues` gives the Hermitian version.

anchors.snippet: The trace of A , defined as the sum of its diagonal elements, is also the sum of all eigenvalues

annotations.label: Determinant equals product of eigenvalues

anchor.snippet: The determinant of A is the product of all its eigenvalues

annotations.note: Also `Matrix.det_eq_prod_roots_charpoly` for algebraically closed fields.

anchors.snippet: The determinant of A is the product of all its eigenvalues

annotations.label: Eigenvalues of matrix powers

anchor.snippet: The eigenvalues of the k th power of A

annotations.note: `HasEigenvalue.pow` shows `(f^n).HasEigenvalue (μ^n)`.

annotations.label: Invertibility iff nonzero eigenvalues

anchor.snippet: The matrix A is invertible if and only if every eigenvalue is nonzero

annotations.note: `spectrum.zero_notMem_iff` states `IsUnit a ↔ 0 ∉ spectrum R a`.

annotations.label: Hermitian matrices have real eigenvalues

anchor.snippet: If A is equal to its conjugate transpose A ∗ , or equivalently if A is Hermitian , then every eigenvalue is real

annotations.note: `IsHermitian.eigenvalues : n → ℝ` returns real eigenvalues; `eigenvalues_mem_spectrum_real` puts them in `spectrum ℝ A`.

annotations.label: Definiteness gives sign of eigenvalues

anchor.snippet: If A is not only Hermitian but also positive-definite , positive-semidefinite, negative-definite, or negative-semidefinite

annotations.note: `PosSemidef.eigenvalues_nonneg` and `IsHermitian.posSemidef_iff_eigenvalues_nonneg` cover the positive case.

annotations.label: Unitary matrix eigenvalues have absolute value one

anchor.snippet: If A is unitary , every eigenvalue has absolute value

annotations.note: `Unitary.spectrum_subset_circle` and `spectrum.norm_eq_one_of_unitary` give |λ|=1 for unitary elements in C*-algebras, applying to unitary matrices.

anchor.snippet: the eigenvalues of matrix I + A (where I is the identity matrix) are

annotations.note: Power case is `HasEigenvalue.pow`; the spectral-mapping statement for general polynomials of `A` (incl. `I+A`) is not stated in this exact form.

annotations.label: Right eigenvector

anchor.snippet: the word "eigenvector" in the context of matrices almost always refers to a right eigenvector

annotations.note: Mathlib's `HasEigenvector` is the right-eigenvector convention `f x = μ • x`.

anchors.snippet: the word "eigenvector" in the context of matrices almost always refers to a right eigenvector

annotations.label: Left eigenvector

anchor.snippet: Any row vector u satisfying this equation is called a left eigenvector of A

annotations.note: No dedicated `leftEigenvector`; obtained as a right eigenvector of the transpose.

anchors.snippet: Any row vector u satisfying this equation is called a left eigenvector of A

annotations.label: Left and right eigenvectors share eigenvalues

anchor.snippet: the left and right eigenvectors of A are associated with the same eigenvalues

annotations.note: `charpoly_transpose` shows `Aᵀ` and `A` have the same characteristic polynomial, hence the same eigenvalues.

annotations.label: Matrix and transpose share eigenvalues

anchor.snippet: A matrix has the same eigenvalues as its transpose, as can be directly seen as follows

annotations.note: `(Aᵀ).charpoly = A.charpoly`, so by `mem_spectrum_iff_isRoot_charpoly` they share eigenvalues.

annotations.label: Eigendecomposition / diagonalizable matrix

anchor.snippet: This is called the eigendecomposition and it is a similarity transformation

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes the spectral/diagonalization theorem for Hermitian matrices; no general `IsDiagonalizable` predicate.

annotations.label: Diagonalizability iff eigenvectors form a basis

anchor.snippet: It then follows that the eigenvectors of A form a basis if and only if A is diagonalizable

annotations.note: Captured via semisimplicity / eigenspaces spanning; no explicit `diagonalizable_iff_eigenvectors_basis` lemma.

annotations.label: Defective matrix and Jordan normal form

anchor.snippet: A matrix that is not diagonalizable is said to be defective

annotations.note: Generalized eigenspaces and `JordanChevalley` decomposition exist; full Jordan normal form and `defective` predicate are not formalized.

annotations.label: Eigenvector-spanned subspaces are invariant

anchor.snippet: Any subspace spanned by eigenvectors of T is an invariant subspace of T

annotations.note: Each eigenspace is an invariant submodule; closure under `⨆` preserves invariance.

annotations.label: Spectrum of an operator

anchor.snippet: in functional analysis eigenvalues can be generalized to the spectrum of a linear operator T

annotations.note: `spectrum R a` defined for any element of an `R`-algebra.

annotations.label: Weights in representation theory

anchor.snippet: The representation-theoretical concept of weight is an analog of eigenvalues

annotations.note: Weights and weight spaces are defined in the Lie algebra library.

annotations.label: Hecke eigensheaf

anchor.snippet: Hecke eigensheaf is a tensor-multiple of itself and is considered in Langlands correspondence

annotations.note: Geometric Langlands / Hecke eigensheaves not in Mathlib.

annotations.note: Abel–Ruffini is formalized for polynomials; not specialized to eigenvalues.

annotations.label: Rotation has complex eigenvalues

anchor.snippet: The characteristic equation for a rotation is a quadratic equation with discriminant

annotations.note: Specific eigenvalue computation for 2D rotation matrices not located.

annotations.label: Squeeze mapping has reciprocal eigenvalues

anchor.snippet: A linear transformation that takes a square to a rectangle of the same area (a squeeze mapping ) has reciprocal eigenvalues

annotations.note: Squeeze mapping concept not formalized.

annotations.label: Principal component analysis

anchor.snippet: This orthogonal decomposition is called principal component analysis (PCA) in statistics

annotations.note: PCA not formalized.

annotations.label: Eigenvalue of a graph

anchor.snippet: In spectral graph theory , an eigenvalue of a graph is defined as an eigenvalue of the graph's adjacency matrix

annotations.note: Spectral graph theory (graph eigenvalues) not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Principal eigenvector of a graph

anchor.snippet: The k -th principal eigenvector of a graph is defined as either the eigenvector corresponding to the k -th largest or k -th smallest eigenvalue of the Laplacian

annotations.note: Not formalized.

annotations.label: Perron–Frobenius theorem for Markov chains

anchor.snippet: The Perron–Frobenius theorem gives sufficient conditions for a Markov chain to have a unique dominant eigenvalue

annotations.note: Perron–Frobenius theorem not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Undamped vibration eigenvalue problem

anchor.snippet: Eigenvalue problems occur naturally in the vibration analysis of mechanical structures

annotations.note: Physical application, not formalized.

anchors.snippet: Eigenvalue problems occur naturally in the vibration analysis of mechanical structures

annotations.label: Principal axes from inertia tensor

anchor.snippet: the eigenvectors of the moment of inertia tensor define the principal axes of a rigid body

annotations.note: Physical application, not formalized.

annotations.label: Stress tensor principal components

anchor.snippet: the stress tensor is symmetric and so can be decomposed into a diagonal tensor with the eigenvalues on the diagonal and eigenvectors as a basis

annotations.note: Physical application, not formalized.

annotations.label: Time-independent Schrödinger equation

anchor.snippet: An example of an eigenvalue equation where the transformation T is represented in terms of a differential operator is the time-independent Schrödinger equation in quantum mechanics

annotations.label: Orientation tensor in geology

anchor.snippet: The output for the orientation tensor is in the three orthogonal (perpendicular) axes of space

annotations.note: Geological application, not formalized.

anchor.snippet: The value R 0 is then the largest eigenvalue of the next generation matrix

annotations.note: Epidemiology, not formalized.

annotations.label: Eigenfaces
